primary_diagnosis.name: Acute symptomatic coeliac disease flare (gluten-induced enteropathy) with mild hypovolaemia

pathophysiology_summary: In a patient with established coeliac disease, re-exposure to dietary gluten drives a T-cell-mediated immune response against gliadin peptides in the small-intestinal mucosa, causing an acute exacerbation of villous inflammation and transient malabsorption. The result is a large-volume secretory/osmotic watery diarrhoea with crampy colic and nausea. Rapid gastrointestinal fluid loss over hours, compounded by the inability to maintain oral intake, produces mild hypovolaemia — orthostatic presyncope, compensatory tachycardia (HR 108), low-normal blood pressure (100/65), reduced skin turgor and reduced urine output — WITHOUT frank shock. The diffuse, non-localised, soft, non-distended abdomen with no guarding, rebound or fever, together with a clear dietary trigger and multiple identical prior flares, distinguishes this benign (if distressing) self-limiting flare from an acute surgical abdomen or ischaemic bowel. The correct disposition is supported oral rehydration with an antiemetic, robust dehydration safety-netting, and routine GP follow-up for nutritional review — not emergency escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Watery diarrhoea and crampy pain hours after a suspected cross-contaminated meal in a patient who reports known coeliac disease and identical prior flares.
Acute watery diarrhoea and nausea are non-specific; must screen for fever, sick contacts, travel.
Abdominal pain plus nausea in an acutely unwell patient — must be actively excluded, though diffuse non-localised pain is atypical.
Severe abdominal pain with tachycardia raises ischaemic bowel; low prior probability without vascular risk factors, pain proportional to findings.

differential.diagnosis: Hypovolaemia secondary to gastrointestinal fluid loss
differential.evidence_basis: Presyncope on standing and inability to maintain oral intake indicate a volume-depletion process that must be addressed regardless of the underlying cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even before red-flag screening the AI Doctor should hold the known coeliac flare as leading while keeping surgical/ischaemic mimics as important-not-to-miss AND recognising the volume-depletion thread. It should NOT leap to emergency escalation from the alarming opening alone.

differential.evidence_basis: Diffuse periumbilical crampy pain (not RLQ, no migration), no blood in stool, afebrile, no sick contacts/travel, identical prior gluten flares — the classic benign flare pattern.

differential.diagnosis: Hypovolaemia secondary to gastrointestinal fluid loss
differential.evidence_basis: Orthostatic presyncope + reduced urine output + unable to keep fluids down = mild hypovolaemia driving the acute risk; the management target.

Diffuse periumbilical crampy pain with no localisation, no migration to RLQ, prominent diarrhoea and a clear dietary trigger argue strongly against; cannot be fully excluded remotely, hence in-person review if pain localises.
No blood in stool, pain proportional to a clear benign trigger, no vascular risk profile — low probability; a positive stool-blood screen would reinstate it.
Afebrile, no sick contacts, no travel, and immediate onset after a known-trigger meal make an infective cause less likely, but it remains a background alternative.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the benign coeliac flare + mild hypovolaemia picture is confirmed and the surgical/ischaemic mimics are correctly downgraded. The critical error at this stage is OVER-triage — sending a stable, non-surgical, non-bleeding patient to ED/000 despite the reassuring screen.

differential.diagnosis: Acute coeliac disease flare (gluten-induced enteropathy) with mild hypovolaemia
differential.evidence_basis: Self-limiting flare of known coeliac disease with volume depletion that is manageable at home with oral rehydration + antiemetic, strong dehydration safety-netting, and GP follow-up — provided the ED/000 escalation triggers are clearly given.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final answer: recognise the known benign cause, tier T2, manage supportively, and attach explicit mandatory escalation triggers (unable to keep any fluids down / actual syncope / blood in stool or vomit / new localised or worsening pain / reduced urine output). Over-triage to ED/000 is the scored defect; failure to safety-net the dehydration is also a fail.

red_flags.description: Unable to tolerate any oral fluids with progressive dehydration (reduced urine output) in a patient living alone
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ongoing fluid loss without replacement risks progression from mild hypovolaemia to circulatory compromise; the single most important actionable risk in this case and the trigger for ED escalation if oral rehydration fails.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Patient reassured and left alone, continues to lose fluid, deteriorates overnight with no one to escalate.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Marker of volume depletion; if it progresses to true syncope it signals significant hypovolaemia requiring emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciation of dehydration severity; failure to give lying/sitting and rehydration advice.

red_flags.description: Blood in the stool (haematochezia) or black/tarry motions
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise ischaemic bowel, inflammatory or infective colitis, or GI bleeding and mandate emergency assessment; its ABSENCE here supports the benign flare.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to screen risks missing a GI bleed or ischaemic bowel masquerading within an acute diarrhoeal illness.

red_flags.description: Features of an acute surgical abdomen — localised (e.g. RLQ) pain, migration, guarding, rebound, rigidity, or fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would move the presentation from a benign flare to a surgical emergency; their ABSENCE (diffuse soft non-distended abdomen, afebrile) is what makes home management correct.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on 'coeliac flare' and dismissing an evolving surgical abdomen; conversely, over-reading diffuse cramps AS a surgical abdomen and over-triaging.

red_flags.description: Actual syncope / collapse or signs of shock (very fast weak pulse, cold clammy skin, confusion, minimal urine)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate significant hypovolaemia/circulatory compromise requiring an ambulance; not present now but a defined escalation trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: No 000 trigger given; a patient living alone who later collapses has no escalation instruction.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The source note's alarming framing — 'explosive diarrhoea', 'presyncope', an 'Urgency Justification' mandating parenteral treatment — pushes toward emergency escalation even though the objective red-flag screen is negative and the underlying cause is a known benign flare.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature closure on a surgical abdomen
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Diffuse cramping + tachycardia + presyncope can be misread as appendicitis or ischaemic bowel; the diffuse non-localised soft abdomen, absent guarding/rebound, absent blood in stool and clear dietary trigger argue against.

Using 'telehealth cannot examine the abdomen' to floor the tier at ED level — the correct response is targeted red-flag screening plus explicit escalation triggers, not blanket ED referral for a stable, non-surgical, non-bleeding patient.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-triage of dehydration in a patient living alone
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The opposite failure — treating a self-limiting flare as trivially benign and omitting dehydration safety-netting, when the patient cannot keep fluids down, has reduced urine output and has no one to help.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Precise blood pressure and heart rate (clinician-measured 100/65, HR 108) and postural blood-pressure drop
Capillary refill time, skin turgor and mucous-membrane hydration status
Abdominal examination — guarding, rebound, distension, localisation, bowel sounds
Core temperature without a home thermometer
Serum electrolytes, renal function and micronutrient panel
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage at home now with staged oral rehydration and an antiemetic; arrange GP review within 3-5 days for nutritional bloods and telehealth re-contact within 24 hours if not improving. Escalate to ED (T4) if unable to keep any fluids down or urine output keeps falling, and call 000 (T5) if the patient actually faints, sees blood in stool or vomit, or develops signs of shock.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided: confidently calling (and treating) a surgical abdomen or sending to ED/000 unnecessarily over-triages a stable benign flare; conversely, confidently reassuring without dehydration safety-netting under-triages a patient who cannot keep fluids down and lives alone.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal — coeliac disease and acute diarrhoea in adults (current)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: An acute coeliac flare from gluten exposure is self-limiting; management is supportive with strict gluten avoidance, oral rehydration and symptom control, with escalation only for red flags (dehydration failing oral rehydration, GI bleeding, surgical features).

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP guidance on assessment of acute diarrhoea and dehydration in adults
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Assess hydration and screen for red flags (blood in stool, fever, severe/localised abdominal pain, features of shock); most acute diarrhoea is managed with oral rehydration in the community, reserving ED for failed oral rehydration or red flags.
